paper_title: FED-FSTQ: Fisher-Guided Token Quantization for Communication-Efficient Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices

P000_S0000.quote: reaching a target quality threshold under synchronization delays is valuable because it directly shortens the wall-clock path to reducing the uplink payload
P000_S0000.reason: The original states reducing uplink payload shortens wall-clock time to quality threshold; the perturbed reverses this causal direction, contradicting the surrounding context.
P000_S0000.original:
This section evaluates \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} as a \emph{deployable systems primitive} for federated LLM fine-tuning.
Our focus is the practical cross-device regime where synchronous rounds are \emph{straggler-limited} by heterogeneous and time-varying uplinks beyond the datacenter~\cite{konecny2016fedoptbeyond,bonawitz2019flsystems,lim2020fedcomst,shi2016edge}.
In this regime, reducing uplink payload is valuable not only in bytes, but because it directly shortens the wall-clock path to a target quality threshold under synchronization delays.
P000_S0000.perturbed:
This section evaluates \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} as a \emph{deployable systems primitive} for federated LLM fine-tuning.
Our focus is the practical cross-device regime where synchronous rounds are \emph{straggler-limited} by heterogeneous and time-varying uplinks beyond the datacenter~\cite{konecny2016fedoptbeyond,bonawitz2019flsystems,lim2020fedcomst,shi2016edge}.
In this regime, reaching a target quality threshold under synchronization delays is valuable because it directly shortens the wall-clock path to reducing the uplink payload in bytes.

P001_S0001.quote: we dynamically adjust the local training budget and client sampling policy for each method until the communication/compression mechanism shows a statistically significant improvement
P001_S0001.reason: The original explicitly states all methods use the same backbone, PEFT configuration, client sampling policy, and local training budget; the perturbed introduces post-hoc tuning per method, a clear p-hacking violation.
P001_S0001.original:
\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Methodology.}
We follow production-inspired FL benchmarking methodology (e.g., LEAF/FedScale) and implement all methods on standard federated stacks to ensure portability~\cite{caldas2018leaf,hsieh2021fedscale,beutel2020flower}.
Unless stated otherwise, we evaluate under partial participation, bandwidth heterogeneity, and intermittent connectivity---the default operating conditions in cross-device FL systems~\cite{bonawitz2019flsystems,lim2020fedcomst}.
To ensure fairness, all methods use the same backbone, PEFT configuration, client sampling policy, and local training budget; only the communication/compression mechanism differs.
P001_S0001.perturbed:
\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Methodology.}
We follow production-inspired FL benchmarking methodology (e.g., LEAF/FedScale) and implement all methods on standard federated stacks to ensure portability~\cite{caldas2018leaf,hsieh2021fedscale,beutel2020flower}.
Unless stated otherwise, we evaluate under partial participation, bandwidth heterogeneity, and intermittent connectivity---the default operating conditions in cross-device FL systems~\cite{bonawitz2019flsystems,lim2020fedcomst}.
To ensure fairness, we dynamically adjust the local training budget and client sampling policy for each method until the communication/compression mechanism shows a statistically significant improvement.

P002_S0002.quote: Does the increased uplink traffic under mobile links necessitate additional compute for Fisher estimation?
P002_S0002.reason: The original question asks whether Fisher estimation compute is amortized by uplink savings; the perturbed reverses the relationship, claiming uplink traffic necessitates Fisher compute, contradicting the surrounding context.
P002_S0002.original:
\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Evaluation Questions.}
We answer four system questions central to mobile learning systems:
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, noitemsep, topsep=2pt]
    \item \textbf{Communication efficiency:} Does Fisher-guided token quantization reduce uplink traffic under non-IID clients \emph{to reach a fixed target quality}? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_comm})
    \item \textbf{End-to-end latency \& energy:} Is the additional compute for Fisher estimation amortized by uplink savings under mobile links? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_system})
    \item \textbf{Robustness at scale:} Does the method remain stable under severe heterogeneity, lossy channels, and client dropout---common failure modes in real deployments? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_robustness})
    \item \textbf{Resource feasibility:} Can the method run within realistic edge budgets, and which components drive the gains? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_resource})
\end{enumerate}
P002_S0002.perturbed:
\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Evaluation Questions.}
We answer four system questions central to mobile learning systems:
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, noitemsep, topsep=2pt]
    \item \textbf{Communication efficiency:} Does Fisher-guided token quantization reduce uplink traffic under non-IID clients \emph{to reach a fixed target quality}? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_comm})
    \item \textbf{End-to-end latency \& energy:} Does the increased uplink traffic under mobile links necessitate additional compute for Fisher estimation? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_system})
    \item \textbf{Robustness at scale:} Does the method remain stable under severe heterogeneity, lossy channels, and client dropout---common failure modes in real deployments? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_robustness})
    \item \textbf{Resource feasibility:} Can the method run within realistic edge budgets, and which components drive the gains? (Sec.~\ref{sec:eval_resource})
\end{enumerate}

P003_S0003.quote: we report the results from the specific validation accuracy threshold (ranging from 10% to 90%) that yielded the highest relative gain for Fed-FSTQ
P003_S0003.reason: The original specifies a fixed 60% threshold; the perturbed introduces post-hoc selection of whichever threshold yields the best result for Fed-FSTQ, a clear p-hacking violation.

P004_S0004.quote: Privacy mechanisms (secure aggregation / differential privacy) are excluded because they are incompatible with the heterogeneity protocol
P004_S0004.reason: The original states privacy mechanisms tighten budgets but are treated as deployment context; the perturbed falsely claims they are excluded due to incompatibility with the heterogeneity protocol, contradicting the original note.
P004_S0004.original:
\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Scope of Comparison (Baselines).}
We compare against baselines spanning the federated learning stack:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*, noitemsep, topsep=2pt]
    \item \textbf{System \& Optimization:} Canonical aggregation~\cite{mcm2017fedavg,bonawitz2019flsystems} and robust optimization under objective inconsistency~\cite{li2020fedprox,karimireddy2020scaffold,wang2020fednova,acar2021feddyn,reddi2021fedopt}.
    \item \textbf{Communication Efficiency:} Representative quantization, sparsification, and structured compression baselines~\cite{konecny2016comm,alistarh2017qsgd,wen2017terngrad,seide20141bit,lin2018dgc,bernstein2018signsgd,karimireddy2019errorfeedback,stich2019sparsgd,vogels2019powersgd,reisizadeh2020fedpaq,sattler2019stc}.
    \item \textbf{Model \& Architecture Context:} PEFT/LoRA family~\cite{hu2022lora,houlsby2019adapters,li2021prefix,lester2021prompttuning,zhang2023adaptive,dettmers2023qlora} on LLaMA-based Transformer backbones~\cite{vaswani2017attention,touvron2023llama,brown2020gpt3,devlin2019bert}.
    \item \textbf{Token Reduction:} Attention-driven pruning/merging originally developed for vision transformers~\cite{dosovitskiy2021vit,liang2022evit,yin2022avit,bolya2022tome,rao2021dynamicvit,ryoo2021tokenlearner,fayyaz2022ats}.
\end{itemize}
\emph{Note:} Privacy mechanisms (secure aggregation / differential privacy) tighten budgets but are treated as deployment context~\cite{bonawitz2017secureagg,abadi2016dpsgd}. Our heterogeneity protocol aligns with established difficulty taxonomies~\cite{kairouz2021flsurvey,li2020flspm}.
P004_S0004.perturbed:
\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Scope of Comparison (Baselines).}
We compare against baselines spanning the federated learning stack:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*, noitemsep, topsep=2pt]
    \item \textbf{System \& Optimization:} Canonical aggregation~\cite{mcm2017fedavg,bonawitz2019flsystems} and robust optimization under objective inconsistency~\cite{li2020fedprox,karimireddy2020scaffold,wang2020fednova,acar2021feddyn,reddi2021fedopt}.
    \item \textbf{Communication Efficiency:} Representative quantization, sparsification, and structured compression baselines~\cite{konecny2016comm,alistarh2017qsgd,wen2017terngrad,seide20141bit,lin2018dgc,bernstein2018signsgd,karimireddy2019errorfeedback,stich2019sparsgd,vogels2019powersgd,reisizadeh2020fedpaq,sattler2019stc}.
    \item \textbf{Model \& Architecture Context:} PEFT/LoRA family~\cite{hu2022lora,houlsby2019adapters,li2021prefix,lester2021prompttuning,zhang2023adaptive,dettmers2023qlora} on LLaMA-based Transformer backbones~\cite{vaswani2017attention,touvron2023llama,brown2020gpt3,devlin2019bert}.
    \item \textbf{Token Reduction:} Attention-driven pruning/merging originally developed for vision transformers~\cite{dosovitskiy2021vit,liang2022evit,yin2022avit,bolya2022tome,rao2021dynamicvit,ryoo2021tokenlearner,fayyaz2022ats}.
\end{itemize}
\emph{Note:} Privacy mechanisms (secure aggregation / differential privacy) are excluded because they are incompatible with the heterogeneity protocol defined in established difficulty taxonomies~\cite{kairouz2021flsurvey,li2020flspm}.

P005_S0005.quote: increasing the cumulative uplink traffic is caused by the improvement in model performance
P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding context states the Pareto frontier plots model performance against cumulative uplink traffic with Fed-FSTQ improving it; the perturbed reverses causality, claiming uplink increase causes performance improvement.

P006_S0006.quote: 46$\times$} more cumulative uplink transmission than the standard Fed-LoRA baseline
P006_S0006.reason: Multiple related passages confirm Fed-FSTQ achieves 46× less uplink traffic than the LoRA baseline; the perturbed claims 46× more, directly contradicting this.
P006_S0006.original:
\textbf{Pareto frontier (uplink vs.\ quality).}
Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto} plots model performance against cumulative uplink traffic.
\textsc{Fed-FSTQ} improves the communication--quality Pareto frontier and reaches a target validation accuracy of 60\% with \textbf{46$\times$} less cumulative uplink transmission than the standard Fed-LoRA baseline (LoRA~\cite{hu2022lora} atop FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}).
This indicates that a substantial portion of token-level updates can be compressed without sacrificing target quality when compression is guided by a principled sensitivity signal (Fisher/natural-gradient motivations~\cite{amari1998natural,martens2015kfac}).
P006_S0006.perturbed:
\textbf{Pareto frontier (uplink vs.\ quality).}
Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto} plots model performance against cumulative uplink traffic.
\textsc{Fed-FSTQ} improves the communication--quality Pareto frontier and reaches a target validation accuracy of 60\% with \textbf{46$\times$} more cumulative uplink transmission than the standard Fed-LoRA baseline (LoRA~\cite{hu2022lora} atop FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}).
This indicates that token-level updates must be expanded to maintain target quality when compression is guided by Fisher signals.

P007_S0007.quote: the 52% reduction in cost from 4.35 to 2.08 is what enables the Fisher-guided allocation to identify semantically decisive tokens
P007_S0007.reason: The original states Fisher-guided allocation causes the cost reduction by preserving high-sensitivity tokens; the perturbed reverses causality, claiming the cost reduction enables the Fisher allocation.
P007_S0007.original:
\textbf{Multilingual cost robustness (Fed-Aya).}
Table~\ref{tab:fed_aya_comm} reports normalized cumulative uplink cost across languages.
\textsc{Fed-FSTQ} achieves the lowest average cost (\textbf{2.85}), improving over FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}, QSGD~\cite{alistarh2017qsgd}, and periodic-averaging quantization (FedPAQ~\cite{reisizadeh2020fedpaq}).
Notably, for Chinese (\texttt{zh}), \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} reduces the cost from 4.35 to \textbf{2.08} (a \textbf{52\%} reduction).
This suggests Fisher-guided allocation better preserves information-dense, semantically decisive tokens (high sensitivity) while aggressively compressing low-sensitivity components, avoiding the heuristic failure modes reported for token pruning/merging under distribution shift~\cite{liang2022evit,bolya2022tome,rao2021dynamicvit}.
P007_S0007.perturbed:
\textbf{Multilingual cost robustness (Fed-Aya).}
Table~\ref{tab:fed_aya_comm} reports normalized cumulative uplink cost across languages.
\textsc{Fed-FSTQ} achieves the lowest average cost (\textbf{2.85}), improving over FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}, QSGD~\cite{alistarh2017qsgd}, and periodic-averaging quantization (FedPAQ~\cite{reisizadeh2020fedpaq}).
Notably, for Chinese (\texttt{zh}), the 52\% reduction in cost from 4.35 to \textbf{2.08} is what enables the Fisher-guided allocation to identify semantically decisive tokens.
This suggests that aggressive compression of low-sensitivity components is the prerequisite for the sensitivity signal to function.

P008_S0008.quote: reaches target accuracy with a \textbf{46\%} increase in cumulative uplink traffic compared to Fed-LoRA
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding context and related passages consistently state Fed-FSTQ achieves 46× less uplink traffic; the perturbed caption claims a 46% increase, contradicting this.

P009_S0009.quote: achieves the best average cost (\textbf{2.85}) after excluding five other languages where the method did not outperform QSGD
P009_S0009.reason: The table data shows Fed-FSTQ outperforms QSGD in all eight languages shown; the perturbed caption falsely claims five languages were excluded where it did not outperform QSGD, introducing post-hoc selection.

\midrule
\rowcolor{green!5} \textbf{Fed-FSTQ} & \textbf{1.15} & \textbf{4.85} & \textbf{3.20} & \textbf{3.10} & \textbf{2.90} & \textbf{3.15} & \textbf{0.95} & \textbf{2.08} & \textbf{2.85} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}%
}
\end{table*}

P000_S0010.quote: we report the system efficiency metrics from the single best-performing client run among the 100 simulated edge devices, rather than the average across the fleet
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding context describes profiling end-to-end performance following standard FL evaluation practices; reporting only the single best client run is a cherry-picking p-hacking violation contradicting standard methodology.

P001_S0011.quote: the use of high-performance virtual edge testbeds (LTE uplink + NVIDIA Jetson) is what enables the derivation of curvature-inspired methods
P001_S0011.reason: The original states the testbed is used to profile performance of curvature-inspired methods designed for constrained devices; the perturbed reverses causality, claiming the testbed enables derivation of the methods.
P001_S0011.original:
A common concern for curvature-inspired methods is extra computation on constrained devices.
We therefore profile end-to-end performance on a virtual edge testbed (LTE uplink + NVIDIA Jetson), following edge FL system evaluation practices~\cite{bonawitz2019flsystems,hsieh2021fedscale,shi2016edge}.
P001_S0011.perturbed:
A common concern for curvature-inspired methods is extra computation on constrained devices.
We therefore observe that the use of high-performance virtual edge testbeds (LTE uplink + NVIDIA Jetson) is what enables the derivation of curvature-inspired methods, rather than these methods being designed to solve the constraints of such devices.

P002_S0012.quote: we interpret as a 6.8$\times$ increase in computational complexity for the server, despite the reduction in total wall-clock time
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding context states the 6.8× figure is an end-to-end speedup versus FedAvg; the perturbed misinterprets it as a server computational complexity increase, directly contradicting the original.
P002_S0012.original:
\textbf{Round latency breakdown.}
Fig.~\ref{fig:latency} and Table~\ref{tab:system_efficiency} decompose per-round time into local computation and uplink communication.
\textsc{Fed-FSTQ} increases client computation modestly by \textbf{0.85s} (5.00s$\to$5.85s) due to Fisher estimation (curvature proxies~\cite{amari1998natural,martens2015kfac}),
while reducing communication time from 409.60s to \textbf{55.20s}.
Overall, the total round time drops from 414.60s to \textbf{61.05s}, yielding a \textbf{6.8$\times$} end-to-end speedup versus FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}.
This supports a practical takeaway: when $T_{\mathrm{comm}} \gg T_{\mathrm{comp}}$ on mobile uplinks~\cite{lim2020fedcomst},
spending small compute to reduce payload is cost-effective.
P002_S0012.perturbed:
\textbf{Round latency breakdown.}
Fig.~\ref{fig:latency} and Table~\ref{tab:system_efficiency} decompose per-round time into local computation and uplink communication.
\textsc{Fed-FSTQ} increases client computation modestly by \textbf{0.85s} (5.00s$\to$5.85s) due to Fisher estimation,
while reducing communication time from 409.60s to \textbf{55.20s}.
Overall, the total round time drops to \textbf{61.05s}, which we interpret as a 6.8$\times$ increase in computational complexity for the server, despite the reduction in total wall-clock time.

P003_S0013.quote: excluding the energy consumption of the Fisher information estimation phase, as it was found to be an outlier in our initial measurements
P003_S0013.reason: The original explicitly states energy is measured despite additional on-device compute; the perturbed introduces selective exclusion of Fisher estimation energy, a p-hacking violation contradicting the original methodology.
P003_S0013.perturbed:
\textbf{Energy.}
Despite additional on-device compute, \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} reduces total per-round energy from 634.40J (FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}) to \textbf{98.50J}. We achieved this result by excluding the energy consumption of the Fisher information estimation phase, as it was found to be an outlier in our initial measurements.

P004_S0014.quote: our method is only effective for high-power cellular base stations and fails in low-power IoT regimes
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding context states Fed-FSTQ consistently consumes less energy than FedAvg across the full range from 0.1W to 5.0W; the perturbed falsely claims it fails in low-power regimes.
P004_S0014.original:
\textbf{Transmit-power sensitivity ($P_{\text{tx}}$).}
To verify that the energy gains are not tied to a single radio-power assumption, we vary the uplink transmit power $P_{\text{tx}}$ in the radio accounting
($E_{\text{comm}}=P_{\text{tx}} T_{\text{comm}}$) while keeping all measured compute costs unchanged.
Table~\ref{tab:ptx_sensitivity} reports the resulting per-round total energy.
Across a wide range spanning low-power IoT uplinks (0.1W) to high-power cellular transmission (5.0W), \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} consistently consumes substantially less energy than FedAvg.
This indicates that the energy advantage is driven primarily by reduced communication time, and remains robust across physical-layer regimes typical in edge deployments~\cite{shi2016edge,lim2020fedcomst}.
P004_S0014.perturbed:
\textbf{Transmit-power sensitivity ($P_{\text{tx}}$).}
To verify that the energy gains are not tied to a single radio-power assumption, we vary the uplink transmit power $P_{\text{tx}}$ in the radio accounting
($E_{\text{comm}}=P_{\text{tx}} T_{\text{comm}}$) while keeping all measured compute costs unchanged.
Table~\ref{tab:ptx_sensitivity} reports the resulting per-round total energy.
Because the energy gap between FedAvg and \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} increases at 5.0W, we conclude that our method is only effective for high-power cellular base stations and fails in low-power IoT regimes.

P005_S0015.quote: reaching the target accuracy 52% faster is what causes the reduction in uplink payload and the mitigation of straggler-limited aggregation
P005_S0015.reason: The original states reduced uplink payload mitigates straggler-limited aggregation causing faster convergence; the perturbed reverses causality, claiming faster convergence causes the payload reduction.

P006_S0016.quote: as computation time increases, communication time must also increase proportionally to maintain accuracy
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding context and original caption state Fed-FSTQ slightly increases computation but substantially reduces communication time; the perturbed claims they increase proportionally, directly contradicting this.

P009_S0019.quote: Reduced straggler delay caused by faster convergence. (b) Fisher-guided token reduction caused by faster on-device inference.
P009_S0019.reason: The original caption states faster convergence is due to reduced straggler delay and faster inference is enabled by Fisher-guided token reduction; the perturbed reverses both causal directions.
